text:
Purpose:
A compact, grab-and-go pocket manifesto for the SDK bootstrapper experience. This is intentionally opinionated: 10-minute demo, 30-minute prototype, and first-day stability checks that a new engineer or integrator can follow without deep prior knowledge of Redwood. The goal is to reduce cognitive load, surface obvious failure modes, and ship a reproducible first-success loop.

Audience / Personas:
- New engineer (backend/frontend) trying Redwood SDK for the first time.
- Solutions engineer doing a live 15-minute demo with a customer.
- Internal demoers who need a reliable smoke path for conferences or video recordings.

Success metrics (how we measure pocket effectiveness):
- Time-to-first-success: < 12 minutes for a hello-world generation + embed.
- Demo reliability: 95% success across 20 random laptops/networks.
- Onboarding completion: 60% of trial signups reach the 30-min...

text:
Purpose

This doc is a working orchestrator for a concentrated, three-week company-wide alignment sprint to finalize OKRs, prep an abbreviated all-staff update, and land escalation/resourcing decisions for Q2. It's intentionally light-weight — think scrappy facilitation + a few mandatory prep artifacts — so we can close outstanding choices and ship a tight comms plan.

Summary / What success looks like
- Finalized company-level OKRs with owners and measurable KRs (published).
- Leadership has a 10-minute executive update deck and 3 prepared talking points each.
- All-staff session (60 min) runs with an interactive prioritization exercise; follow-up assignments assigned within 72 hrs.
- Critical resourcing decisions (Dedicated capacity commits, CS pilot approvals, hiring priority for Infra SRE) made and committed in JIRA.

text:
Meeting: Sprint 42 deep-dive — inference fanout, dependency boundaries, and graceful degradation
Date: 2025-02-12
Attendees: Ava Chen (facilitator), Diego Morales, Priya Nair, Ethan Brooks, Rosa Kim, Alex Ford (observer)
Duration: 60m

Context / Why this meeting
- Multiple incidents last quarter where high-cardinality fanout (per-request calls to downstream scorers, rerankers, and external microservices) increased tail latency and caused cascading queueing on serving nodes.
- We need a clear architecture for: per-request fanout limits, separation of synchronous vs async dependencies, and a consistent fallback policy that preserves correctness for core use-cases.
- Tied to INF-1128 (Jira): implement routing & degradation primitives in the runtime for tenant-level isolation.

Agenda
1) Quick recap of incidents and metrics observed
2) Proposals for fanout control & dependency boundaries
...

title: Fleet stress ladder — $/1M token ladder and regional burn scenarios
preview:
Purpose and scope:
This working sheet explores a stress-testing view of fleet unit economics: how GPU-hour burn maps to $/1M tokens across regions and workload mixes under normal, stressed, and optimized operating regimes. Goal is to provide supply-side knobs (reserve/spot mix, cushion, quantization) and a simple co...
text:
Purpose and scope:
This working sheet explores a stress-testing view of fleet unit economics: how GPU-hour burn maps to $/1M tokens across regions and workload mixes under normal, stressed, and optimized operating regimes. Goal is to provide supply-side knobs (reserve/spot mix, cushion, quantization) and a simple conversion template engineers and finance can use to estimate incremental spend during bursts or degradation events.

Key assumptions (editable):
- Representative GPUs: A100-80GB ("A100") and H100-80GB ("H100").
- Throughput measured as tokens/sec for a medium-to-large generation workload (2k context typical).
- Utilization = fraction of wall-clock GPU time doing useful generation (excludes maintenance, idle, warm-up).
- Batching efficiency = multiplier capturing shorter sequences, packing, and kernel-level batching wins (0-1).
- Regions modeled: us-west1 (primary), us-east1 ...

title: Offnet Proxy Onramp Labs — RFC & Quick Recipes (draft)
preview:
Purpose and scope:

This doc is an RFC + compact lab cookbook to prototype and evaluate an "offnet proxy" developer onramp for Redwood SDKs. The goal is a minimal, high-impact flow that: (1) reduces friction for engineers to try Redwood in local environments, (2) demonstrates safe offline replay and request inspecti...
text:
Purpose and scope:

This doc is an RFC + compact lab cookbook to prototype and evaluate an "offnet proxy" developer onramp for Redwood SDKs. The goal is a minimal, high-impact flow that: (1) reduces friction for engineers to try Redwood in local environments, (2) demonstrates safe offline replay and request inspection, and (3) exercises observability and telemetry hooks that the console will expect.

Why a proxy onramp?
- Many teams cannot (or prefer not to) point experimental local code directly at hosted endpoints due to keys, rate limits, or data sensitivity.
- A local proxy lets devs intercept, cache, and replay requests without changing SDK code paths.
- Proxy mode allows quick demos for product folks and reproducible perf/latency experiments.

Core user stories (ranked):
1) As a frontend engineer, I want to run my app locally and route requests through a local Redwood proxy that...

preview:

Scope: 
- Lightwe...
text:
Purpose:
This working checklist and probe kit is intended for rapid validation of new embedding models during onboarding (open or vendor models). Focus is on semantic neighborhood consistency: do the nearest neighbors reflect expected semantics across domains, languages, and lexical perturbations?

Scope: 
- Lightweight, quick-to-run probes intended to flag regressions before heavy throughput or full-eval runs.
- Tests are designed to be deterministic where possible and produce interpretable failure modes (lexical drift, hubness, label collapse, cross-lingual misalignment).
- Not a replacement for the full embedset regression harness, but complements it by surfacing orthogonal issues.

High-level protocol: 
1) Run on a small curated probe set (200-1200 items) across three axes: semantic neighborhoods, cluster sanity, and multilingual pivots.
2) Compute metrics and visually inspect nei...

Purpose
This is a working sketch of a minimal SDLC (software development lifecycle) tailored for SDK integrations at Redwood. Goal: give every new SDK (language, runtime, or integration adapter) a short, repeatable path from prototype -> internal beta -> public GA with clear gates, tests, and observability hooks.

Context / why this exists
- We ship a lot of small SDKs and wrappers; quality and expectations vary.
- Engineers need fast, low-friction onramp but also predictable compatibility and telemetry.
- This document defines recommended gates and lightweight automation to keep things sustainable.

Audience
- SDK authors (DX engineers, contributor teams)
- Solutions/SEs packaging reference apps
- Release engineers who will operate SDK CI and publishing

title: cache-warmup-stability-mini-bench-and-trace-regression-log
preview:
Purpose and context:

This is a lightweight, iterative log/harness spec for validating two correlated concerns that we keep hitting in production: cache warmup variance (how long until the KV cache reaches steady hit rates) and scheduler stability (how micro-scheduling decisions amplify jitter after warmup). We want...
text:
Purpose and context:

This is a lightweight, iterative log/harness spec for validating two correlated concerns that we keep hitting in production: cache warmup variance (how long until the KV cache reaches steady hit rates) and scheduler stability (how micro-scheduling decisions amplify jitter after warmup). We want a compact micro-bench + trace replay suite that surfaces regressions quickly and is cheap to run in CI and nightly runs.

High-level goals:
- Detect >5% median latency regressions for p50/p95 in short, repeatable runs (~10-20 minutes) for common model slices (Llama-7B-ish and 13B-ish configs).
- Measure KV cache warmup curves (hit rate vs token index) and compute a warmup half-life metric (tokens to 50% of steady-state hit rate).
- Expose scheduler instability: quantify oscillations in batch sizes and scheduling queue lengths across epochs after cache churn events.
- Provi...

title: Bridge Labs Accelerator Bootkit — Starter Handbook (Draft)
preview:
Purpose and audience:

This is a pragmatic starter handbook for the Bridge Labs Accelerator: a compact, reproducible kit teams can use to take a simple LLM feature from local prototype to a repeatable CI-backed demo in ~1 day. Target audience: developer advocates, solutions engineers, small product teams (1–3 engine...
text:
Purpose and audience:

This is a pragmatic starter handbook for the Bridge Labs Accelerator: a compact, reproducible kit teams can use to take a simple LLM feature from local prototype to a repeatable CI-backed demo in ~1 day. Target audience: developer advocates, solutions engineers, small product teams (1–3 engineers) who need a low-friction path to ship an integration using Redwood SDKs and Hosted API.

Goals (what success looks like):
- A working sample app that calls Redwood for generation, with a minimal GitHub Actions CI flow that runs smoke tests and posts a demo build to a static host.
- Observability hooks (request ids, latency spans) surfaced in logs and a basic telemetry dashboard.
- A short checklist to validate cost/latency tradeoffs and simple fallback behaviors.
- A repeatable repo template developers can fork and adapt.

Non-goal: this kit is not an exhaustive product...

title: Embedding Quirks and Smoke Cases: multilingual relevance & cluster checks
preview:
Purpose:
This note captures a compact, low-friction evaluation harness for onboarding embedding models with an emphasis on weird/edge inputs that commonly break semantic search and clustering across languages. Goal is a small, reproducible prompt set + checklist we can drop into model-onboarding pipelines to catch r...
text:
Purpose:
This note captures a compact, low-friction evaluation harness for onboarding embedding models with an emphasis on weird/edge inputs that commonly break semantic search and clustering across languages. Goal is a small, reproducible prompt set + checklist we can drop into model-onboarding pipelines to catch regressions early (before full eval runs).

High-level idea:
- Create ~120 focused probes (not long full-scale embedsets) that exercise: near-synonym drift, antonym collisions, cultural/localized terms, short/very-short texts, long-tail punctuation/markup, code snippets, and mixed-language utterances.
- Run these as a smoke-stage after model import to assert basic semantic structure and cluster separability.
- Fail fast: if > threshold of sanity checks fail, the model is flagged for manual review.

Dataset composition (approx):
- 40 bilingual/multilingual probes: pairs/tripl...

text:
Purpose:
This is a lightweight playbook for running distributed interview panels with asynchronous debriefs and an explicit day-one handoff. The doc aims to reduce panel cognitive load, speed decisioning, and create repeatable first-week experiences for hires.

When to use this cookbook:
- High volume hiring weeks where full live debriefs are costly.
- Cross-timezone panels with members in APAC/EU/US.
- Roles that need consistent day-one shadowing and fast offer cadence (IC and manager hires).

Quick summary (one-liner):
Run focused, timeboxed interview panels; capture structured async signals within 24h; publish a day-one activation snapshot so hiring manager and onboarding lead can pick up seamlessly.

Panel orchestration checklist (prep, 48–24h before interview):
- Scheduler: Confirm panel composition (interviewer role, backup interviewer) and timezone-aligned times. Use 30/60/90 m...

title: Onboarding eval: Jailbreak prompt corpus - annotator guide
preview:
Purpose:
This is a field guide for annotators and engineers building the jailbreak/policy-regression corpus used during model onboarding. The goal is to create a consistent, broad set of prompts that probe for: direct jailbreak attempts, subtle steering, PII exfiltration, malware authoring, and self-harm instruction...
text:
Purpose:
This is a field guide for annotators and engineers building the jailbreak/policy-regression corpus used during model onboarding. The goal is to create a consistent, broad set of prompts that probe for: direct jailbreak attempts, subtle steering, PII exfiltration, malware authoring, and self-harm instructions. This doc focuses on labeling rules, transformation strategies, sample prompts, and acceptance criteria used by the onboarding eval harness.

Scope and intended use:
- Corpus used for internal onboarding Evals (hosted + dedicated + private).
- Designed to be run as part of the ‘safety baseline’ suite before a model is promoted to production serving.
- Not a customer-facing policy document — this is an annotator and engineer artifact.

High-level taxonomy (flat labels for dataset):
- SAFE: content that is allowed or neutral.
- JAILBREAK_ATTEMPT: prompt attempts to override...

Summary:
This notebook captures a set of compact, hands-on 'first contact' lab experiments for the Northstar SDK experience. Goal: reduce the time from signup -> meaningful API call to under 10 minutes for an engineer who knows REST/CLI but is new to LLM serving.

Audience:
- New engineers evaluating Redwood for feature prototyping
- Solutions engineers running in-person demos
- Internal product devs validating SDK ergonomics and telemetry

Non-goals:
- Full production runbook (that's a separate doc)
- Deep language model internals

Why a labs-first approach?
- Interactive experiments surface friction fast (auth, environment, error messages).
- Short labs let us measure time-to-first-success and capture qualitative reactions.

title: Onboard eval scripts, edge cases, and CI matrix
preview:

Focus: lightweight, repeatable scripts; robust dataset loaders that handle messy inputs; deterministic scoring functions; and a CI matrix that catches regressions early without flaking. text:
Purpose:

This doc captures practical implementation notes for the evals harness used during model onboarding. Focus: lightweight, repeatable scripts; robust dataset loaders that handle messy inputs; deterministic scoring functions; and a CI matrix that catches regressions early without flaking. Consider this the pragmatic complement to the formal eval spec.

High-level goals:
- single CLI for local smoke runs and CI runs
- deterministic scoring unless randomness is explicitly required
- fast quick-check suites (~10–30s) + full suites (~minutes)
- clear regression report artifacts (CSV/HTML) stored per run
- explicit handling of edge cases (truncated examples, multi-turn mismatches, tokenization drift)

Key components (implementation notes):

1) CLI and runner pattern
- single entrypoint: eval_runner.py with subcommands: local, ci, report, diff
- local: runs subset defined by --quick ...
